nativeRange.habitat: Shallow sandy and silty substrates near inner reef lagoons, coastal waters, mangroves, and large tidal pools at depths of 0–18 m

decorPreferences: PVC pipe or cave structures acceptable

diet.notes: Primarily micropredatory; has been documented accepting frozen mysis shrimp, enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped clam or prawn, sinking marine carnivore pellets, and copepods. Generally advised to feed 2–3 times daily using target feeding near the burrow entrance, particularly for newly introduced specimens.

avoidWith: other similar-shaped gobies in small tanks
small ornamental shrimps that may be preyed upon

summary: A stocky, pale-bodied watchman goby with distinctive pink to red spots, generally kept singly or as a mated pair. Known for forming symbiotic burrow-sharing relationships with pistol shrimps in nature and in captivity.
careNotes: The Lagoon Shrimp Goby (Cryptocentrus leptocephalus) tends to spend most of its time perched at or near the entrance of a self-excavated burrow in sandy substrate. A soft, deep sandy substrate is generally considered essential. It has been observed burrowing beneath live rock structures or using buried PVC pipes as a ready-made shelter. A tight-fitting lid is strongly recommended, as this species has been known to jump from open aquariums. It is generally considered safe with corals and sessile invertebrates; however, isolated cases of predation on small ornamental shrimps and tiny bristleworms have been reported, so caution is advised in reef tanks with small crustaceans. In most cases it is compatible with a wide range of reef fish, though it tends to be aggressive toward conspecifics and similarly shaped gobies unless kept as an established mated pair. Captive-bred specimens are commonly reported to acclimate more readily and tend to accept prepared foods more willingly than wild-caught individuals. The species has been observed forming symbiotic partnerships with pistol shrimps of the genus Alpheus (particularly Alpheus bellulus and Alpheus randalli) in the home aquarium, with the shrimp maintaining the burrow while the goby acts as a lookout. The species is frequently sold under the trade names 'Pink Spotted Watchman Goby', 'Pink Spot Goby', and (notably on LiveAquaria) 'Spotted Watchman Goby' — all referring to the same species (Cryptocentrus leptocephalus). It is also occasionally listed as 'Singapore Shrimp Goby' or 'Pink & Blue Spotted Goby' to highlight the small blue spots that ring the larger pink/red spots on its head and body.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has been documented. A mated pair generally requires a larger aquarium (commonly cited at 150 L or more) with adequate sandy substrate. Both parents have been reported to guard the egg clutch. The larvae are noted as relatively hardy compared to other marine gobies, though rearing through the planktonic larval stage remains challenging in home aquariums.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: LiveAquaria collections page did not list Cryptocentrus leptocephalus at the time of access; species-specific data sourced from secondary and tertiary sources below.

additional.name: TFH Magazine
additional.notes: Primary species article. Confirmed adult size 10–15 cm, native range Western Pacific Indonesia to New Caledonia to Australia, reef-safe status, burrow behavior, and carnivore diet. Used as effective primary source for this entry.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed max size 12 cm SL, temperature range 24.7–29.2°C (mean 28.6°C), native range Western Pacific Indonesia to New Caledonia/Yaeyama Islands/NW Australia, taxonomy Gobiiformes/Gobiidae. Cross-verified size and range.

additional.name: AquaticCommunity
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size 15 cm, temperature 24–28°C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025. Tank size given as 20 gal (76L) minimum for single specimen; 30 gal (114L) recommended with tankmates. Noted as more aggressive toward conspecifics than many shrimp gobies. Notes predation on small shrimp.

additional.name: iReef UK
additional.notes: Confirmed size 10–13 cm, temperature 24–27°C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025. Minimum tank 100 L. Lifespan 5–7 years. Care level intermediate. Cross-verified temperature and salinity parameters.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size 12 cm, minimum tank 100 L, Western Pacific range, carnivore diet, escape risk (noted as jumper). Salinity ~1.026 noted but in line with standard reef parameters. Cross-verified size and tank minimum.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding documented; mated pairs will spawn in sufficiently sized aquariums with sandy substrate. Rearing larvae through the planktonic stage is generally considered challenging in the home aquarium.
